retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The off-label indications of metformin include managing gestational diabetes, addressing weight gain issues caused by antipsychotic medication, preventing type 2 diabetes, and treating and preventing polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Metformin is currently the only ADA-recommended antidiabetic medication used for prediabetes. [2] Furthermore, researchers are studying metformin for its potential antiaging, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin, an antidiabetic agent, was approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in 1994 for treating type 2 diabetes. This medication comes in both immediate- and extended-release formulations and is often combined with other antidiabetic agents. The off-label indications of metformin include managing gestational diabetes, addressing weight gain issues caused by antipsychotic medication, preventing type 2 diabetes, and treating and preventing polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS). Metformin is currently the only antidiabetic medication for prediabetes recommended by the American Diabetes Association (ADA). Furthermore, regarding potential indications, researchers are studying metformin for its possible antiaging, anticancer, and neuroprotective effects. This activity offers a comprehensive review of metformin's indications, contraindications, and adverse effects. In addition, it emphasizes the crucial role played by the interprofessional team in effectively managing patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is a biguanide compound used as first-line therapy for the treatment of diabetes mellitus and prevention of diabetic-related microvascular and macrovascular complications. [1] It is also used to treat several other conditions, including polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), hyper-insular obesity, and weight gain from antipsychotic therapy. It is a derivative of Galega officinalis , also known as French lilac or goat’s rue. It was used as an herbal remedy in Medieval Europe and found in the 1920s to contain guanidine, an anti-hyperglycemic compound. [2] Phenformin, metformin’s predecessor, was the first oral biguanide that was withdrawn from the market for a high incidence of fatal lactic acidosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: The distribution volume: The distribution volume for the 850 mg strength of metformin is approximately 654 ± 358 L.

retrieved_docs.title: Aliskiren (Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: O'Rawe et al conducted a phase I clinical trial to determine the safety and tolerability of several drugs that target the renin-angiotensin system in patients with glioblastoma. [12] The glioblastoma stem cells contain elements of the renin-angiotensin system. Thus, inhibiting the renin-angiotensin system in glioblastoma stem cells might have anticancer effects. Patients who relapsed from standard treatment, eg, surgery, radiation, and temozolomide, and met inclusion criteria received oral doses of aliskiren, aspirin, celecoxib, cilazapril, curcumin/piperine, metformin, and propranolol. Ten patients out of 17 completed the pharmacotherapy regimen. Two patients reported mild adverse effects. The median survival was 19.9 months compared with 14.6 months for standard therapy. O'Rawe et al state that this difference is not statistically significant. However, the trend of greater survival time for relapsed patients receiving drugs that target the renin-angiotensin system is a promising lead.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma protein binding: Metformin exhibits negligible plasma protein binding.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Elimination: Metformin is excreted unchanged in the urine, with no significant hepatic metabolism or biliary excretion.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Elimination half-life: The elimination half-life of metformin is approximately 6.2 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Steady-state plasma concentration: Steady-state plasma concentration is reached within 24 to 48 hours.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Bioavailability: Under fasting conditions, the bioavailability of the metformin hydrochloride 500 mg tablet is 50% to 60%.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin, classified as a biguanide drug, effectively lowers blood glucose levels by decreasing glucose production in the liver, diminishing intestinal absorption, and enhancing insulin sensitivity. As a result, metformin effectively lowers both basal and postprandial blood glucose levels. Metformin plays a crucial role in PCOS by reducing insulin levels, which leads to decreased luteinizing hormone and androgen levels. This normalization of hormone levels helps regulate the menstrual cycle in women. Clinicians must advise premenopausal women about the increased potential for pregnancy while taking metformin. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin-Associated Lactic Acidosis (MALA) -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: There are two subsets of metformin toxicity: Chronic or incidental MALA - a predisposing pathophysiological condition that leads to supra-therapeutic accumulation of metformin Intentional or acute metformin-induced lactic acidosis (MILA) – an acute overdose of metformin

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin is recognized as weight-neutral, with the potential to induce modest weight loss. Moreover, the drug is unlikely to cause hypoglycemia and may have potential cardioprotective effects, further adding to its value in diabetes treatment. [3] Metformin typically takes approximately 3 hours to take effect after administration, and its half-life is about 20 hours. Metformin is not significantly metabolized in the liver nor exhibits substantial protein binding. Instead, it is primarily eliminated through the kidneys, mostly unchanged. Hence, monitoring renal function is crucial when using metformin to ensure safe and effective treatment. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Hypoglycemic Medications -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Metformin increases hepatic adenosine monophosphate-activated protein kinase activity, thus reducing hepatic gluconeogenesis and lipogenesis and increasing insulin-mediated uptake of glucose in muscles.

retrieved_docs.title: Metformin -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: In case of any concerns, it is vital to inform all clinical team members to ensure everyone has access to the same information and operates from a unified data set. This coordinated interprofessional approach, with open communication and shared decision-making between healthcare providers, fosters optimized therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: and the insulin secretagogue repaglinide. The “biguanide blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs” subclass comprises fenformin and metformin, with metformin explicitly identified as a biguanide. Lipid‑modifying therapy is represented by the plain fibrate class, which contains bezafibrát, klofibrát and gemfibrozil. Bezafibrát and klofibrát each produce decreased lipolysis, contributing to reduced circulating free fatty acids and improved lipid profiles. Gemfibrozil is linked to the formulation group “G [Preparations]” as its parent preparation and is contraindicated when co‑administered with repaglinide, highlighting a clinically important drug‑interaction risk in patients receiving both lipid‑lowering and glucose‑lowering treatments. Collectively, these hierarchical classifications delineate the specific agents within each diabetes‑related class, their molecular targets and physiologic effects, and key therapeutic interactions that inform safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2068
retrieved_docs.content: Fenformin is classified as a member of the biguanide blood glucose‑lowering drugs, a drug class that acts to reduce elevated blood glucose levels. Recognizing fenformin’s membership in this biguanide class clarifies its mechanism of action and its therapeutic role in the management of hyperglycemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 112
retrieved_docs.content: organized under “blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins” and include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors (alogliptin, sitagliptin), and GLP‑1 analogues (liraglutide, lixisenatide). A further subclass, biguanides, comprises metformin and fenformin, which share a common chemical scaffold and glucose‑lowering activity. These hierarchical relationships illustrate how glycerol is shared across diverse formulations and how diabetes medications are systematically categorized by mechanism, molecular target, and pharmacologic profile to guide clinical use and patient management.

This group includes METFORMIN (the prototypical biguanide) and its misspelled variant FENFORMIN, both of which act by decreasing hepatic glucose production and improving insulin sensitivity. Overall, the taxonomy links insulin‑based injectable therapies, GLP‑1 receptor agonists, alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, and biguanides under the broader umbrella of diabetes pharmacotherapy, highlighting their hierarchical relationships and the ways in which combination products integrate agents from different subclasses to achieve comprehensive glycemic control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 873
retrieved_docs.content: Alpha glucosidase inhibitors, blood glucose lowering and biguanide blood glucose lower drugs are both classified as blood glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins, indicating that they belong to the broader category of non‑insulin agents used to reduce blood glucose levels. Within the biguanide blood glucose lower drugs class, the specific agents metformin and fenformin are members, highlighting that these individual drugs are examples of biguanide‑type glucose‑lowering medications. Together, these drug classes and their constituent agents represent important therapeutic options for managing hyperglycemia in diabetes without the use of insulin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 34
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES is a broad therapeutic category that encompasses both insulins and analogues as well as blood glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Within the non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents, Alpha glucosidase inhibitors, blood glucose lowering are classified as a type of blood glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins. Similarly, biguanide blood glucose lower drugs are also a subclass of blood glucose‑lowering drugs, excl. insulins. The biguanide class includes individual agents such as fenformin and metformin, which are members of the biguanide blood glucose lower drugs group. Together, these relationships delineate the hierarchical organization of diabetes medications, distinguishing insulin‑based therapies from various oral glucose‑lowering agents, including alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors and biguanides like metformin and fenformin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 721
retrieved_docs.content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES encompass two major categories: insulins and analogues, and blood glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. The non‑insulin blood glucose‑lowering drugs form a distinct subclass of diabetes medications and include several pharmacologic groups. Alpha glucosidase inhibitors, blood glucose lowering are classified as a type of blood glucose‑lowering drug that excludes insulins. Biguanide blood glucose lower drugs also belong to this non‑insulin blood glucose‑lowering category, and metformin is a representative member of the biguanide class. This hierarchical organization distinguishes insulin‑based therapies from oral and other agents that lower glucose through different mechanisms, aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment strategies for patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 812
retrieved_docs.content: DISEASES OF THE ENDOCRINE PANCREAS INCLUDING DIABETES is classified as a subgroup of other endocrine disorders and encompasses several specific conditions. Among its child entities are مرض السكري دون مضاعفات (diabetes without complications), نقص تركيز السكر (hypoglycemia), اضطراب إفراز الجسترين (gastrin‑secretion disorder), فرط أنسولين الدم (hyperinsulinemia), and ورم غلوكاغوني (glucagonoma). Hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر) is recognized as a manifestation of pancreatic endocrine disease and, in the broader endocrine context, is considered a related disorder within the spectrum of other endocrine disorders. The gastrin‑secretion disorder (اضطراب إفراز الجسترين) can lead to downstream gastric pathology, specifically الحثل وإضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ (gastric mucosal atrophy and associated secretion abnormalities). Pharmacologically, fenformin (metformin) is associated with a relationship to hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر), reflecting its glucose‑lowering effect and the potential for low blood‑sugar episodes in susceptible patients. Together, these interconnections illustrate how diseases of the pancreatic endocrine system are nested within the larger category of endocrine disorders, how individual pancreatic conditions influence related metabolic and gastrointestinal outcomes, and how therapeutic agents such as fenformin interact with these pathophysiological processes.
